figures.caption: Figure 1: Comparison among traditional deep learning methods, zero-shot methods, and the proposed FMG-Pan framework in terms of performance, efficiency, and generalization. FMG-Pan achieves a better trade-off by leveraging pretrained models for fast, image-specific adaptation without modifying model architecture or requiring external training data.
Figure 8: Time comparison (in seconds) between the proposed FMG-Pan framework (with LAGNet and FusionMamba as pretrained models) and previous zero-shot methods (PsDip, ZS-Pan) on WV3 and WV2 data.

Figure 13: Comparison of efficiency and reconstruction quality on WV3. (a) Runtime, (b) GPU memory usage (c) HQNR. FMG achieves substantially lower runtime and memory consumption compared to Full and LoRA, while maintaining competitive reconstruction quality.
Figure 11: Visual fusion images and the related HQNR maps on a full resolution QB example.
Figure 10: Visual fusion images and the related HQNR maps on a full resolution GF2 example.
Figure 7: Comparison of HQNR for FusionMamba and LAGNet with and without the FMG-Pan framework across two real-world datasets (WV3,WV2). Note: * denotes LAGNet, without * denotes FusionMamba.

Figure 2: Overall framework of the proposed FMG-Pan framework. The pretrained model and the PF module are first executed independently. Subsequently, the parameters of the adaptive model are optimized using three core loss functions: pseudoreference loss, spectral loss, and physical loss. In the reasoning stage, the final HRMS image is generated by applying the trained adaptive model to the input PAN and LRMS images.

Figure 3: Flowchart of the PF module. At the downsampled resolution, the coefficient matrix H is obtained by solving Eq. (14). The reconstructed LRMS Ŷ is then multiplied with H along mode-3 to generate the reference detail image for guiding the fusion process.

Table 5: Comparison in terms of HQNR, 𝐷𝜆 , 𝐷𝑠 , and runtime (s), of FMG-PanFusionMamba, its lighter-weight variant (FMGPanL FusionMamba), and the fine-tuned variant (FMG-PanF FusionMamba) on 20 full resolution test images for both WV3 and WV2

Figure 4: Comparison of two adaptive models. (1) Standard conv backbone with residual blocks. (2) Lightweight variant using depthwise separable convolutions and fewer channels for reduced complexity. Model (1) is used by default. DWConv(·, ·) is the depthwise separable convolution and Conv(·, ·, ·) is the standard convolution.
